source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
70
   After citing the Dalai Lama in an apolitical matter and posting it on Instagram, Daimler encountered
   severe criticism from China. Daimler responded by deleting the post and said it sincerely accepted all
   criticism and would take every action to increase its “understanding of China’s culture and values.”
   Sui-Lee Wee, ‘Mercedes-Benz Quotes the Dalai Lama. China Notices. Apology Follows’ New York
   Times (6 February 2018), online: www.nytimes.com/2018/02/06/business/mercedes-daimler-dalai-
   lama-china.html. Mercedes-Benz is only one example, ‘Delta Air Lines and Zara were reprimanded
   by authorities for listing Taiwan and Tibet as countries on their websites.’ Pei Li and Adam
   Jourdan, ‘Mercedes-Benz apologizes to Chinese for quoting Dalai Lama’ Reuters (6 February 2018),
   online: www.reuters.com/article/us-mercedes-benz-china-gaﬀe-idUSKBN1FQ1FJ.
71
   Gary King, Jennifer Pan and Margaret E Roberts, ‘How the Chinese Government Fabricates Social
   Media Posts for Strategic Distraction, not Engaged Argument’ (2017) 111(3) American Political
   Science Review 484. In recent years a change in the social media landscape has taken place which
   led to party and government microblogs replacing formerly inﬂuential bloggers. See Creemers (n
   49) 93.
72
   Not in the case of blacklists, but for example, in the local models and the envisioned uniﬁed system.
73
   Some of these questions are answered here: Liang and others (n 9) 421. Yet, this analysis is not com-
   prehensive of the entire SCS as the required information is only available for some agencies collecting,
   aggregating and analysing data.
74
   See Fei Shen (n 37) 30.
75
   See Knight (n 37) 251–2.
76
   See European Union Chamber of Commerce in China (n 13) 12.
77
   See Creemers (n 49) 97.
                                                          TRANSNATIONAL LEGAL THEORY                 305
